did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Partial Least Squares Discriminant Analysis (PLS-DA), a statistical machine learning method, to classify Raman and autofluorescence spectra for the diagnosis of skin cancer (melanomas, basal cell carcinomas, and benign tumors). The classification models were validated using 10-fold cross-validation.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is labeled as a 'REGULAR ARTICLE' and reports original experimental results from an in vivo study involving 617 cases of skin neoplasms. It details the implementation of a portable Raman spectroscopic device, describes the methods for spectral data collection and classification using PLS-DA, and presents new diagnostic accuracy metrics (ROC AUCs, sensitivity, specificity) compared to general practitioners and dermatologists.

title: In vivo diagnosis of skin cancer with a portable Raman spectroscopic device
brief_summary: This study evaluated an in vivo diagnosis of skin cancer using a portable, low-cost Raman spectroscopic device that combines Raman and autofluorescence spectra analysis in the near-infrared region. The researchers analyzed 617 cases of skin neoplasms, including melanomas, basal cell carcinomas, and benign tumors, collected from patients at a specialized oncology dispensary. Spectral data were classified using Partial Least Squares Discriminant Analysis (PLS-DA) with 10-fold cross-validation to ensure model stability. The system achieved ROC AUCs of 0.75 for distinguishing malignant from benign tumors, 0.69 for melanomas versus pigmented tumors, and 0.81 for melanomas versus seborrheic keratosis. The diagnostic accuracy of the automated system was found to be higher than that of general practitioners and trainees, and comparable to or slightly lower than that of trained dermatologists. The authors conclude that this low-cost portable approach is suitable for mass screening and can be combined with dermoscopy to improve classification accuracy.

lesion_types_studied.details: 70 cases included in the study cohort; used in classification tasks against benign tumors, pigmented tumors, and seborrheic keratosis.
122 cases included in the study cohort; grouped with melanoma and SCC as malignant tumors.
12 cases included in the study cohort; grouped with melanoma and BCC as malignant tumors.
413 cases included in the study cohort; used as the comparator group for malignant tumors in the 'Malignant vs Benign' classification task.
Included in the benign tumor group (n=413 total benign); specific count not isolated in abstract but listed as a class label.
Included in the benign tumor group (n=413 total benign); specific count not isolated in abstract but listed as a class label.
Included in the benign tumor group (n=413 total benign); specific count not isolated in abstract but listed as a class label.
113 cases included in the study cohort; used in classification task 'MM vs SK' and grouped with nevi as 'pigmented tumors' (NE+SK).
Included in the benign tumor group; grouped with seborrheic keratosis as 'pigmented tumors' (NE+SK) for specific classification tasks.

all_task_types.target: Diagnostic categories of skin neoplasms (malignant vs. benign, melanoma vs. pigmented tumors, melanoma vs. seborrheic keratosis)
all_task_types.details: The study uses Partial Least Squares Discriminant Analysis (PLS-DA) to classify Raman and autofluorescence spectra into discrete diagnostic categories: malignant tumors (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma) versus benign tumors; melanomas versus pigmented tumors (nevi and seborrheic keratosis); and melanomas versus seborrheic keratosis. The task is binary or multiclass classification based on histologically confirmed labels.

datasets_used.name: Samara Regional Clinical Oncology Dispensary Cohort
datasets_used.description: A prospective in vivo cohort of patients with suspected skin cancer examined at the Samara Regional Clinical Oncology Dispensary in Samara, Russia. Patients were referred from local clinics by general practitioners. Spectral data (Raman and autofluorescence) were collected in vivo, and lesions were histologically confirmed after excisional biopsy.

use.training: The entire dataset was used for model development with 10-fold cross-validation to check stability; no separate training/test split was reported.
use.validation: 10-fold cross-validation was used for model stability and hyperparameter selection (number of latent variables).
use.testing: 10-fold cross-validation was used for performance evaluation (ROC AUC, sensitivity, specificity); no independent external test set was reported.
use.other: The cohort was also used to calculate biopsy ratios and compare diagnostic accuracy against general practitioners, trainees, and dermatologists.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Classification of skin neoplasms based on Raman and autofluorescence spectral data.
To check the stability of the PLS-DA classification models and to select the number of latent variables (principal components) by minimizing the root mean square error (RMSE).
To evaluate the diagnostic performance of the classification models by calculating the Area Under the Curve (AUC) for different diagnostic tasks (Malignant vs Benign, MM vs NE+SK, MM vs SK).
To estimate the uncertainty of the ROC AUC values.
To perform joint analysis of Raman and autofluorescence signals (Raman+AF) for classification.

statistical_methods_used.method: Standard statistical testing for AUC comparisons
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To test the significance of differences between ROC AUCs of different classification models.

statistical_methods_used.method: Sensitivity, Specificity, Positive Predictive Value (PPV), Negative Predictive Value (NPV)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To quantify the diagnostic accuracy of the automated system at specific thresholds (e.g., 90% sensitivity).
To estimate the clinical utility by calculating the number of negative biopsies per true-positive biopsy at specific sensitivity levels (90%, 95%, 99%).

hypothesis_testing.test: Standard statistical testing for AUC comparisons (referenced as standard manner in citation 39, likely DeLong's test or similar for correlated ROC curves given the context of comparing classifiers on the same dataset), and significance testing of individual AUCs against 0.5.
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: The Area Under the Curve (AUC) of the classification model is equal to 0.5 (no discriminative ability); or there is no significant difference between the AUCs of two compared classifiers.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: The AUC is significantly different from 0.5 (for individual model significance); or there is a significant difference between the AUCs of two compared classifiers.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: All AUCs for the Malignant vs Benign classification were reported as significant with p < 0.001. Comparisons between classifiers (e.g., Raman+AF vs Raman, Full vs Raman+AF) were tested for statistical relevance; differences were found to be not statistically significant (p > 0.1, p > 0.3, p > 0.07, p > 0.5) where reported.

Malignant vs Benign Neoplasms.notes: All AUCs significant (p < 0.001). For Raman+AF at 90% sensitivity: specificity >32%, PPV 40%, NPV 85%, biopsy ratio 1.53:1.

MM vs NE+SK (Pigmented Tumors).notes: For Raman+AF at 90% sensitivity: specificity >26%, PPV 23%, NPV 89%, biopsy ratio 3.32:1. Biopsy ratio for Raman classification of MM and pigmented lesions is 3.95:1 at 90% sensitivity. Biopsy ratio for AF classification is 3.33:1 at 90% sensitivity.

MM vs SK (Seborrheic Keratosis).notes: For Raman+AF at 90% sensitivity: specificity >40%, PPV 48%, NPV 87%, biopsy ratio 1.08:1. Best biopsy ratio for differentiation of MM from SK is 0.92:1.

General Metrics.notes: PPV ranged from 20% to 52%; NPV ranged from 73% to 99%.

Comparison with Clinicians.notes: Accuracy of automatic analysis is higher than GPs and trainees, and comparable or less to trained dermatologists.

architecture_used.input_size: Spectral data in regions: 300–1800 cm⁻¹ (Full), 300–1200 cm⁻¹ (AF), 1200–1800 cm⁻¹ (Raman); normalized using min-max normalization and SNV.

architecture_details.algorithm_implementation: SIMPLS algorithm for PLS part, implemented via MDAtools package in R studio software.
architecture_details.latent_variables: 1 to 4 principal components (PCs) utilized; number of PCs chosen by minimizing root mean square error (RMSE) of 10-fold cross-validation.
architecture_details.variable_importance: Variable Importance in Projection (VIP) scores used to highlight informative spectral bands.

training_details.hyperparameter_selection: Number of latent variables (PCs) selected to minimize RMSE in cross-validation.

classification_or_output_head.method: PLS predictors calculated to present numeric value of neoplasm diagnosis.
classification_or_output_head.joint_analysis: Logistic regression implementation used for joint analysis of AF and Raman parts (Raman+AF model).

preprocessing_techniques: Data centring (subtracting the mean value from each spectrum)
Normalization using Standard Normal Variate (SNV) method (subtracting mean and dividing by standard deviation)
Autofluorescence background subtraction in the 1200–1800 cm⁻¹ range using algorithm by Zhao et al.
conclusions: The proposed portable, low-cost Raman spectroscopic device combining Raman and autofluorescence spectra analysis is suitable for in vivo diagnosis of skin cancer.

The automated diagnostic system achieved ROC AUCs of 0.75 for distinguishing malignant from benign tumors, 0.69 for melanomas versus pigmented tumors, and 0.81 for melanomas versus seborrheic keratosis.
The diagnostic accuracy of the automated system was higher than that of general practitioners and trainees, and comparable to or slightly lower than that of trained dermatologists.

The joint analysis of Raman and autofluorescence signals (Raman+AF) provided the best performance for skin neoplasms classification, particularly for the separation of melanomas and seborrheic keratosis.

Classification of melanomas and pigmented lesions (nevi and seborrheic keratosis) was difficult due to high autofluorescence background from melanin, resulting in lower diagnostic accuracy (ROC AUC 0.69) and higher biopsy ratios.
The system demonstrated promising biopsy ratios (ranging from 0.92:1 to 4.08:1 at sensitivity levels from 99% to 90%), indicating potential clinical utility in reducing unnecessary biopsies.
The low-cost portable approach is considered suitable for mass screening applications to assist first-hand specialists and general practitioners in detecting skin neoplasms.
The proposed approach may be combined with other optical techniques, such as dermoscopy, to increase the accuracy of neoplasm classification.
important_remarks: The study lacks an independent external validation cohort; model stability and performance were assessed solely using 10-fold cross-validation on a single institutional dataset (Samara Regional Clinical Oncology Dispensary, Russia), limiting generalizability.

The device is a low-cost, portable system with a detector operating at -15°C (compared to -60 to -120°C in high-end lab systems), which significantly increases spectrum acquisition time (30–60 seconds vs. 0.1–1 second) and reduces signal-to-noise ratio, particularly affecting Raman signal detection in pigmented lesions.

High autofluorescence background from melanin masks weak Raman peaks, leading to lower diagnostic accuracy for melanomas versus pigmented tumors (ROC AUC 0.69) and higher biopsy ratios (3.32:1 to 3.95:1) compared to non-pigmented classifications.
The cohort includes a small number of Squamous Cell Carcinoma cases (n=12), which may limit the statistical power and reliability of results specific to this malignancy type.

Positive Predictive Values (PPV) are low (ranging from 20% to 52%), indicating a high rate of false positives and potential for unnecessary biopsies despite the system's ability to reduce biopsy ratios compared to general practitioners.

The study compares the system's accuracy against general practitioners and trainees (where it performs better) and trained dermatologists (where it is comparable or slightly worse), but does not provide a direct head-to-head clinical trial comparison with dermatologists using the same device.

The authors acknowledge that the evidence base for comparing portable spectroscopic systems with existing Computer-Aided Diagnosis (CAD) systems is limited due to heterogeneity in populations, devices, and models across studies.

The system's performance for distinguishing melanomas from seborrheic keratosis (ROC AUC 0.81) is lower than that reported in previous studies using high-cost, deeply cooled detectors (e.g., Lui et al. reported AUC 0.90), suggesting potential performance degradation due to hardware limitations.
